text: BRAXTED (LITTLE), a parish in Essex, 1 m. E by N of Witham. Area 1,680 acres. Pop. 126.

BRAY, a lake in co. Wicklow, at the boundary with co. Dublin. A smaller lake lies a little above it, and participates in its name. It is one of the smallest of the Wicklow lakes, and covers only 643 acres; yet, owing to the loftiness, force and uniqueness of its scenery, it possesses considerable interest. Its bed is a crater-like excavation, and has an elevation of 1,225 ft. above sea-level. Upper Lough Bray covers an area of 28 acres, and has an elevation of 1,453 ft.

BRAY, a rivulet in the extreme N of co. Wicklow. It issues from the lake noticed in the preceding article; and runs about 8 m. E. It is not navigable; yet occasionally, when the bar mouth is open, vessels can be carried on a spring-tide to near the bridge of B.
BRAY, a parish in co. Wicklow. Area 2,986 acres. Pop. in 1831, 3,499; in 1841, 3,326. Brayhead, situated nearly 1 m. SE of the town of B., has an alt. above sea-level of 807 ft.

BRAY, a market and post town, partly in the p. of Old Connaught, co. Dublin, but chiefly in the p. of Bray, co. Wicklow. It stands on both sides of the B. rivulet, immediately above the bay of B., 71 m. N of Newtown Mount Kennedy, and 10 m. SSE of Dublin. Pop. in 1831, 3,758; in 1841, 3,185. The town has for many years been a favourite summer-resort of the Dublin citizens; and possesses the various appliances required for their accommodation and comfort whether as lodgers or as tourists. The amount of trade, except for the strictly local market, is very small. BRA

BRAY, a parish in Berks, 2 m. SE of Maidenhead, on the S bank of the Thames, and intersected by the Great Western railway. It comprises the divisions of Bray, Touchen, Water-Oakley, and part of Maidenhead. Area 8,900 acres. Pop. 3,722. B. was famous for a time-serving vicar, who, during the reigns of Henry VIII., Edward VI., Mary, and Elizabeth, changed his religion so as to retain his charge, being twice a protestant, and twice a papist. This unprincipled conduct having attracted notice, he is said to have defended himself on the ground that his ruling principle was "to live and die vicar of Bray."

BRAY, a commune of France, in the dep. of Eure, cant. of Beaumont-le-Roger, 20 m. WNW of Evreux. Pop. 482. It has manufactories of a variety of cotton fabrics and linen.—Also a district of France, in the ancient prov. of Normandy, now comprised in the dep. of the Lower Seine. Its chief towns were Gournay, Forges, Aumale, and Neufchatel. See also BRET.
BRAYBROOKE, a parish in the co. of Northampton, 3 m. SSE of Market-Harborough. Area 3,060 acres. Pop. 420.
BRAYDON, a hamlet in the p. of Purton, Wilts, 45 m. SSW of Cricklade. Pop. 60.

BRAYE, a river of France, which takes its rise in the dep. of Eure-et-Loire, to the SW of Authon, passes Vibray, enters the dep. of Sarthe, and running along the SE confines, unites with the Loire at Songe, and 5 m. SSW of Besse. Its total course does not exceed 45 m., and it is floatable only half that distance.
BRAYFIELD (COLD), a parish in the co. of Buckingham, 3\frac{1}{2} m. E of Olney, on the river Ouse. Area 530 acres. Pop. 83.

BRAY-SUR-SEINE, a canton, commune, and town of France, in the dep. of the Seine-et-Marne, arrond. of Provins. The cant. comprises 24 com., and contained in 1831 a pop. of 11,455. The town is on the l. bank of the Seine, which is crossed by a stone bridge, 11 m. SW of Provins. Pop. 1,992. It has a considerable trade in corn, osiers, and fish, and has two annual fairs.

BRAY-SUR-SOMME, a canton, commune, and town of France, in the dep. of the Somme, arrond. of Péronne. The cant. comprises 20 com., and in 1831 contained a pop. of 11,090. The town is on the r. bank of the Somme, 12 m. W of Peronne. Pop. 1,447. It has extensive tanneries.
BRAYTOFT, or BRATOFT, a parish in the co. of Lincoln, 5½ m. E by S of Spilsby. Area 1,220 acres. Pop. 235.

BRAYTON, a parish in the W. R. of Yorkshire, 13 m. WSW of Selby, intersected by the Leeds and Selby railroad, and the Selby canal. It comprises the township and chapelry of Barlow, and the townships of Brayton, Burn, Gateforth, Hambleton, and Thorpe-Willoughby. Area 10,690 acres. Pop. 1,894.
BRAZACORTA, a town of Spain, in Old Castile, in the prov. and 40 m. SSE of Burgos, on the l. bank of the Pilde.
BRAZEN-HEAD, a promontory of S Africa, on the coast of Kaffraria, dist. of Amapoonda, 19 m. NE of 1st Point of Natal, in S lat. 31° 45', and E long. 29° 14'.
BRAZEY-EN-PLAINE, a commune of France, in the dep. of Côte-d'Or, cant. of Saint-Jean-de- Losne. Pop. 1,618.
